earnings overall, the reaction to

We're in the middle now of first quarter
earning season and it's been better than
expected despite this war underway.
Indeed, Maria. Thank you for having me.
Always a pleasure. Also, the first thing
I'll say on earnings is this is
something you and I have been talking
about for well over a year now in terms
of this structural uptrend in
productivity growth that we're seeing
here in the US economy and it's flowing
through to earnings in a broad base
manner and it's likely to continue over
the medium term.
So, are there areas of this market that
you want to buy on that notion alone
that earnings are strong and the
backdrop for the stock market is is
solid?
Yeah, I think that's We go back to our
source of funds theme that we outlined
last fall in terms of if you have a
technology like AI diffusing throughout
the economy, what we're essentially
transferring is is is productivity and
profitability and margins from the tech
sector to the entire economy. And so
that it behooves us as investors to go
find the businesses that have the lowest
comparative basis from the perspective
of their their revenue and their
profitability and bet on those in terms
of catching up to the tech sector. Not
in absolute terms, but in delta terms,
in rate of change terms and that's what
moves markets.
Yeah, and I want to get your take, EJ,
on where the disruption is happening as
a result of AI. As you know, this is AI
week this morning and we are looking at
how companies are implementing
artificial intelligence. But you know,
when you consider that AI is is is
pushing us to do different things, look
at information and get intelligence in
different ways, you have to believe that
there are going to be losers in that as
well. I'm wondering what you think
are the winners and losers in AI. We
talked with Jack Hidary from Sandbox AQ
yesterday and listen to what he said.
Watch this.
I just want to point out one other thing
that I spoke with somebody yesterday
about this. There will be losers here,
right? I mean, this is a completely new
way of getting information and a new way
of using technology to gather all of
that information. So, it's different
than what we were doing in the dot-com
boom early days in terms of using the
internet. So, those those companies, are
they losers in in all of this, the ones
that we used to rely on 10 years ago?
>> There's two potential big losers in this
kind of economy. First, you have the
legacy software companies, so companies
like SAP and others that we don't see
really innovating, right? When it comes
to Anthropic Claude making it so easy to
code. I was just visiting a number of
companies this week, some customers.
They're using Claude to make their own
software internally and they're not
going to be licensing as much of the
legacy software out there. So, I think
some of the legacy companies have to
really step up and also
>> And the second one is going to be legacy
companies in the big traditional
industries, auto makers, pharma
companies. They've got to get on the
bandwagon.
EJ, your reaction.
I I think that's a really good take.
Look, if you are somebody who is in that
that data data gathering space, AI is
going to be able to do that a heck of a
lot more efficiently than you do on your
own. So again, people using AI are going
to be much, much better suited, I think,
to to remain in that space versus the
people again just trying to to do it
manually. If you want to think of AI,
it's a bit crude, but think of it as as
a glorified search engine. While AI
might not be as good in the creative
space, it's incredibly good at the at at
critiquing and again, the data gathering
and data analysis is where it really,
really shines. Well, I want to get your
take on ServiceNow. The stock is down
sharply this morning. The company said
that the Iran conflict hit subscription
revenue growth.
It still had a solid quarter, it topped
expectations, it raised full year
guidance, but the company said that
there were delays in large Middle East
deals and that they're taking a more
cautious outlook on geopolitical risks
even as it doubles down on its own AI
strategy. ServiceNow
had solid numbers, but there was profit
pressure, lower margins tied to
acquisitions and spending. And the
bigger picture really was that AI
disruption fears and Middle East deal
delays hit sentiment across the the
SaaS SaaS
backdrop, EJ.
Yeah, I think we're we might be
underestimating just how bad the
disruptions from from the Iran war are.
For example, we've seen now, I think,
billions of dollars in terms of planned
infrastructure spend and and CapEx in
the Middle East that has now been put on
hold indefinitely because people are
looking for essentially additional
safety for when the conflict finally
subsides before they're willing to put
money and and lives on the line to
actually get those projects up and
running. Yeah, Taylor, how do you see
things?
I think, you know, you've got
geopolitics hitting some companies, but
I think the larger worry here is AI.
Even as ServiceNow completely changed
its business and is implementing it in a
solid way. You know, the nice thing
about this war in Iran, Maria, is I
think it's really forced the market and
these individual companies to figure out
if that's a bigger deal, if higher oil
prices [snorts] are a bigger deal, or if
earnings, the bottom line, the
fundamentals of their business are a
bigger deal. And I think you're spot on,
it is AI. This is a market that has been
looking at earnings season as the driver
higher or lower. It is not higher oil
prices at this moment. So, this
SaaS-pocalypse, the fear that Anthropic
and AI is coming for every software
company near you. I think a little bit
of that is overblown, but these are
companies that are going to have to be
very smart about how they move forward.
